cl_2023_cdc_11_q_1.instructions:
Directions for questions 1 to 4: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

Reading is a relatively novel addition to the human repertoire — less than 6,000 years old — and the idea that it might be available to everybody is a very recent innovation. For most of our history, our languages were spoken, our literary imaginations oral. In those ancient societies where writing first developed — in Mesopotamia and Mesoamerica, in Egypt and China — both its applications and access to it were restricted. Written language, associated with the rise of states and the spread of commerce, was useful for trade, helpful in the administration of government and integral to some religious practices. Writing was a medium for lawmaking, record-keeping and scripture, and reading was the province of priests, bureaucrats and functionaries. They performed rites, recited poems and circulated information within a narrow, privileged sphere.

For most of history, that is, universal literacy was a contradiction in terms. The Latin word literatus designated a member of the learned elite. A general readership, in the way we understand it now, did not exist, even as a general human ability to read was evident from the start. Anyone could learn to do it, but the mechanisms of learning were denied to most people on the grounds of caste, occupation or gender. According to Steven Roger Fischer’s lively and informative “A History of Reading” (2003), “Western Europe began the transition from an oral to a literate society in the early Middle Ages, starting with society’s top rungs — aristocracy and clergy — and finally including everyone else around 1,200 years later.”

Finally! This transformation gained momentum in 1455, when reading found its killer app in Johann Gutenberg’s printing press. Before that, writing had been done on stone tablets and codices, scrolls of papyrus or animal skin, and bound books that were often copied by hand — objects of necessarily limited circulation. The print revolution catalyzed a global market that flourishes to this day: Books became commodities, and readers became consumers.

For Fischer, as for many authors of long-range synthetic macrohistories, the story of reading is a chronicle of progress, the almost mythic tale of a latent superpower unlocked for the benefit of mankind. “If extraordinary human faculties and powers do lie dormant until a social innovation calls them into life,” he writes, “perhaps this might help to explain humanity’s constant advancement.” “Reading,” he concludes, “had become our union card to humanity.”

This is a beautiful idea, and not one I’m inclined to quarrel with, not even to note that unions can always be broken, and progress stalled or reversed. Humanity, though, is a notoriously gnarled and thorny proposition, and it might be that the history of reading, especially in the post-Gutenberg era, reveals just what complicated and contradictory creatures we have always been.

For one thing, the older, restrictive model of literacy as an elite prerogative proved to be tenacious, even as, in early modern Europe, reading spread among the bourgeoisie, and then further down the social ladder. Nowadays, parents and other concerned adults worry that young people don’t read or love reading enough. Their counterparts in the 18th and 19th centuries were apt to fret that the young loved reading too much.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_1.question_text: ‘Humanity, though, is a notoriously gnarled and thorny proposition, and it might be that the history of reading, especially in the post-Gutenberg era, reveals just what complicated and contradictory creatures we have always been.’ Which of the following best captures the essence of this sentence?
options: The history of reading in the post-Gutenberg era, highlights the complicated and contradictory nature of reading. nature of reading.
The history of reading is grappled with paradoxes marred by the twisted propositions of what we call humanity. call humanity.
The history of reading is not merely a timeline but a tool to reveal the complexities inherent in human nature.
The history of reading reveals the dichotomy of human advancement and the contradictions inherent in humanity. humanity.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_1.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
The original sentence astutely suggests that the history of reading, specifically in the era following Gutenberg's invention of the printing press, provides invaluable insights into the complex and contradictory facets of human nature. While acknowledging the developmental trajectory of reading, the sentence also accentuates that this history is intrinsically tied to the labyrinthine nuances and contradictions of humanity. Thus, Option 3 is the most precise in capturing this multidimensional interpretation. It articulates that the history of reading serves not merely as a chronological record but as an incisive tool to uncover the multi-layered complexities and inherent contradictions that have always defined human nature.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) While this answer acknowledges the 'complicated and contradictory nature of reading,' it somewhat misses the mark by narrowing its focus solely on reading. The original sentence emphasizes that the history of reading reveals the complicated and contradictory nature of humanity, not just of reading itself.
(2) This option uses the phrase 'grappled with paradoxes marred by the twisted propositions of what we call humanity,' which adds a negative tone not present in the original sentence. The original sentence discusses complexity and contradiction but doesn't necessarily frame them as 'twisted propositions.'
(4) While this option comes close to capturing the essence of the original sentence, it falls short by framing the history of reading as a dual narrative of human advancement and inherent contradictions. The original sentence places greater emphasis on the history of reading as a medium that reveals the intricate and contradictory nature of humanity, rather than narrating it as a story of both progress and contradiction.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_2.question_text: Based on the passage, which of the following statements about the role of written language in the early stages of its development is true?
options: It was a tool for the organization and functioning of societies.
People at the time did not have the capacity to learn it.
It was an integral part of religious communication.
The general public did not reap significant benefits from it.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_2.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Your Answer : 3
The passage elaborates that in the early stages of its development, written language had multifaceted applications: it was “useful for trade, helpful in the administration of government, and integral to some religious practices.” These aspects collectively point to the fact that written language was an instrumental tool in the organization and operation of early societies. For example, the passage specifies that written language was used for “lawmaking, record-keeping, and scripture,” showing that it served administrative, legislative, and even spiritual functions. All of these roles can be reasonably inferred as essential for the proper functioning and organization of societies, especially in those days. Hence, option (1) fully aligns with what the passage says, making it the correct answer.

Incorrect Answers:
2. The passage directly contradicts this option. It specifies that “a general human ability to read was evident from the start,” clearly indicating that people had the cognitive capacity to learn written language. The barrier was not a lack of capacity but rather societal restrictions that limited access based on “caste, occupation, or gender.”
3. While the passage does state that written language was “integral to some religious practices,” this was only one among several uses. It was also used for “trade” and “administration of government.” Therefore, describing it as solely or primarily a tool for religious communication would be a misleading oversimplification. The passage emphasizes its multifunctional nature.
4. This option is tricky. While it’s true that access to written language was limited mostly to “priests, bureaucrats, and functionaries,” the passage does not delve into whether or not the general public reaped benefits from it. For instance, effective governance and trade systems facilitated by written records could have had ripple effects benefiting broader society. Therefore, it’s not accurate to conclude definitively that the general public did not reap “significant benefits” based on the passage.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_3.question_text: “If extraordinary human faculties and powers do lie dormant until a social innovation calls them into life,” he writes, “perhaps this might help to explain humanity’s constant advancement.” Which one of the following is not an implication of this statement?
options: Future social innovations can unlock even more extraordinary human faculties.
Limiting access to social innovation would limit human advancement.
Social innovation mentioned in the sentence refers to our ability to read.
Social innovations may not be the only factor contributing to human advancement.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_3.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 4
The statement in the passage suggests that “extraordinary human faculties and powers” lie dormant until they are activated or “brought to life” by a social innovation. In this specific context, the extraordinary faculty is the ability to read, and the social innovation that activated this dormant ability is Gutenberg’s printing press. The phrase “social innovation” does not refer to the faculty itself (“our ability to read”) but rather to the mechanism that enables the activation of this faculty. Therefore, option (3), which states, “Social innovation mentioned in the sentence refers to our ability to read,” is not an implication of the statement in the passage and is the correct answer.

Incorrect Answers:
1. The statement very much leaves room for the idea that other dormant human faculties could be unlocked by future social innovations. If reading—a complex skill—could be widely disseminated thanks to the technological innovation of the printing press, it’s reasonable to infer that future innovations could unlock other extraordinary faculties. It adheres to the core idea that social innovation can activate dormant human capabilities.
2. This choice is a straightforward implication of the statement. If social innovations serve to activate dormant human faculties and thereby contribute to humanity’s constant advancement, then limiting access to such innovations would naturally hamper or limit this advancement. This is an extension of the statement’s underlying principle that social innovation plays a role in unlocking human potential.
4. The original statement says, “perhaps this might help to explain humanity’s constant advancement,” which introduces an element of speculation by using the word “perhaps.” This means that while social innovation is a significant factor in humanity’s advancement, it’s not necessarily the only one. Other factors could also be contributing to the same. Therefore, the statement does imply that other factors might be at play.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_4.question_text: All of the following cannot be inferred from the passage EXCEPT:
options: that reading is an activity whose value, while broadly proclaimed, is hard to specify.
the transition from an oral to a literate society began with Gutenberg’s printing press.
reading too much or too little¯societal anxieties about reading have been a consistent theme.
the elitist nature of reading was nullified as Europe entered the modern period.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_4.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 1
The passage mentions that there is a current concern that young people today don’t read or love reading enough. The fact that the passage addresses both historical significance and current concerns about reading allows us to reasonably infer that societal anxieties about reading, whether it be reading too much or too little, have been a consistent theme throughout history.

Incorrect Answers:
1. The passage does not suggest that reading’s value is “hard to specify.” On the contrary, it explores the historical transformations that reading and the printed word have brought about in society, indicating that its value can be quite clearly outlined, at least in a historical and societal context. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
2. While Gutenberg’s printing press did play a significant role in making reading more accessible, the passage explicitly states that the transition from an oral to a literate society in Western Europe began in the early Middle Ages. This implies that the transition was already underway before Gutenberg’s invention. Hence, the statement is not accurate based on the passage.
4. The passage discusses how the ability to read spread to broader segments of society over time, starting with religious elites and eventually extending to the bourgeoisie. However, it does not state or imply that the “elitist nature of reading was nullified” as Europe entered the modern period. The term “nullified” suggests a complete elimination of elitism, which the passage does not confirm. Even as reading became more widespread, we cannot conclude that its elitist nature was entirely eradicated. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_5.instructions:
Directions for questions 5 to 8: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

The obvious question is, “west of what”? Likewise, where is “the east”? Terms used in present-day geopolitics regularly make reference to an east and west, as in “Far East,” and “Middle East,” as well as in “Western” ideas or attitudes. The obvious answer is that “the West” has something to do with Europe. If the area, including Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq, Israel - Palestine, and Egypt is somewhere called the “Middle” or “Near” East, doesn’t that imply that it is just to the east of something else?

In fact, we get the original term from Greece. Greece is the center-point, east of the Balkan Peninsula was east, west of the Balkans was west, and the Greeks were at the center of their self-understood world. Likewise, the sea that both separated and united the Greeks and their neighbors, including the Egyptians and the Persians, is still called the Mediterranean, which means “sea in the middle of the earth” (albeit in Latin, not Greek ¯ we get the word from a later “Western” civilisation, the Romans). The ancient civilisations clustered around the Mediterranean treated it as the center of the world itself, their major trade route to one another and a major source of their food as well.

To the Greeks, there were two kinds of people: Greeks and barbarians (the Greek word is barbaros). Supposedly, the word barbarian came from Greeks mocking the sound of non-Greek languages: “bar-barbar- bar.” The Greeks traded with all of their neighbors and knew perfectly well that the Persians and the Egyptians, and the Phoenicians, among others, were not their inferiors in learning, art, or political organisation, but the fact remains that they were not Greek, either. Thus, one of the core themes of Western Civilization is that right from its inception, of the east being east of Greece and the west being west of Greece, and of the world being divided between Greeks and barbarians, there was an idea of who is central and superior, and who is out on the edges and inferior (or at least not part of the best version of culture).

In a sense, then, the Greeks invented the idea of west and east, but they did not extend the idea to anyone but themselves, certainly including the “barbarians” who inhabited the rest of Europe. In other words, the Greeks did not have a concept of “Western Civilization,” just Greek vs. barbarian. Likewise, the Greeks did not invent “civilisation” itself; they inherited things like agriculture and writing from their neighbors. Neither was there ever a united Greek empire: there was a great Greek civilisation when Alexander the Great conquered what he thought was most of the world, stretching from Greece itself through Egypt, the Middle East, as far as western India, but it collapsed into feuding kingdoms after he died. Thus, while later cultures came to look to the Greeks as their intellectual and cultural ancestors, the Greeks themselves did not set out to found “Western Civilization” itself.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_5.question_text: It can be inferred that the Greeks:
options: looked down upon their neighbouring civilizations.
considered themselves the originators of Western Civilization.
acknowledged the contributions of neighbouring civilizations.
believed that the “West” was superior to the “East”.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_5.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
The passage makes it clear that the Greeks “traded with all of their neighbors and knew perfectly well that the Persians and the Egyptians, and the Phoenicians, among others, were not their inferiors in learning, art, or political organisation.” This statement directly points to the Greeks acknowledging the contributions and capabilities of their neighbouring civilizations. They traded with these civilizations and recognized their skills in various aspects, such as learning, art, and governance. Therefore, option (3) aligns most closely with the information provided in the passage, making it the correct answer.

Incorrect Answers:
1. While the Greeks did have the term “barbarians” for those who were not Greek, the passage explicitly states that they “knew perfectly well that the Persians and the Egyptians, and the Phoenicians, among others, were not their inferiors in learning, art, or political organisation.” Thus, it would be incorrect to say they looked down upon their neighbouring civilizations.
2. The passage mentions that “the Greeks did not have a concept of ‘Western Civilization,’ just Greek vs. barbarian.” It goes on to say that “the Greeks themselves did not set out to found ‘Western Civilization’ itself.” Therefore, option (2) is not supported by the text.
4. There is no information in the passage to suggest that the Greeks believed the “West” was superior to the “East.” The passage does state that the Greeks considered themselves the center of their world, but it does not delve into their opinions on the relative merits of the “West” versus the “East.” Therefore, option (4) cannot be inferred from the passage and is incorrect.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_6.question_text: What is the significance of the Mediterranean in the context of the overall passage?
options: It acts as a central point in shaping ancient perceptions of geography.
It is presented as the source of the origin of the term “the West”.
It highlights the Greek and Latin influences of the etymology of “Western”.
It exemplifies the cross-cultural exchange that led to understanding “East vs West.”
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_6.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
The passage describes the Mediterranean as a sea that “both separated and united the Greeks and their neighbors, including the Egyptians and the Persians.” Furthermore, it mentions that “the ancient civilizations clustered around the Mediterranean treated it as the center of the world itself, their major trade route to one another and a major source of their food as well.” These details underline the Mediterranean’s crucial role in ancient civilizations’ conceptualizations of geography. It wasn’t just a body of water; it was a connector and a central point around which societies developed their understanding of the world, including their perspectives on trade, culture, and even sustenance. Therefore, option (1) encapsulates the Mediterranean’s importance as outlined in the passage: a central point in shaping ancient perceptions of geography.

Incorrect Answers:
2. The passage does not attribute the origin of the term “the West” to the Mediterranean. The concept of “the West” is said to come from Greece; the Greeks are mentioned as viewing themselves at the “center of their self-understood world,” but the Mediterranean itself is not presented as the source of the term. Hence, option (2) is incorrect based on the information provided in the passage.
3. The term ‘Mediterranean’ does derive from Latin, meaning “sea in the middle of the earth,” but the passage doesn’t focus on this etymological point to illustrate Greek and Latin influences on the concept of “Western.” The Mediterranean’s role is presented more in terms of its importance to various civilizations as a central geographical point, not as an etymological clue to the term “Western.” Therefore, option (3) does not capture the main point regarding the Mediterranean in the passage.
4. The passage mentions that the Greeks traded with their neighbours and acknowledges the importance of this sea as a trade route. However, the passage does not specifically state that this led to a mutual or cross-cultural understanding of “East vs West.” Instead, it underscores the Mediterranean’s role as a central point that helped shape how ancient civilizations like the Greeks viewed their world geographically. While trade is mentioned, the focus is more on its importance as a central geographical point. Thus, option (4) doesn’t fully represent the Mediterranean’s role as described in the passage.

Thus, one of the core themes of Western Civilization is that right from its inception, of the east being east of Greece and the west being west of Greece, and of the world being divided between Greeks and barbarians, there was an idea of who is central and superior, and who is out on the edges and inferior (or at least not part of the best version of culture).

In a sense, then, the Greeks invented the idea of west and east, but they did not extend the idea to anyone but themselves, certainly including the “barbarians” who inhabited the rest of Europe. Likewise, the Greeks did not invent “civilisation” itself; they inherited things like agriculture and writing from their neighbors. cl_2023_cdc_11_q_7.question_text: Each of the following can be deduced from the passage EXCEPT:
options: Greeks can be considered ethnocentric¯ evaluating other cultures using one’s own standards.
there was a lack of political unity in ancient Greece.
a civilization’s view of what is “central” is shaped by geography and culture.
the Greeks considered the civilizations outside of them as barbaric.

Solution:
Correct Answer : 4

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_8.question_text: The passage presents arguments to primarily answer which of the following questions?
options: How did the Greeks contribute to the modern understanding of “East” and “West”?
What is the historical context behind the terms “East” and “West” in geopolitics?
How have geographical terms like “East” and “West” evolved over time?

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_8.solution:
Solution:

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_9.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

Is there any instance of suicide among animals? Yes, said many. The most quoted example of an animal committing suicide is: homo sapiens. Humans are animals, and humans do commit suicide, so animals do commit suicide.

Others have reported examples of pets and domestic animals that starve to death after the passing of their mate, for example, geese. . . . “Suicidal” dogs, starving to death near their owner’s grave, have been known since ancient times, and were reported in modern times, also. “That a dog will grieve itself to death is well documented. Some will refuse food and water after the passing of a close human or animal,” stated an anonymous Nom de Plume. And then, of course, there are the suicidal cows throwing themselves off a cliff near the small village of Lauterbrunnen, in the Alps. . . .

A French investigator, Bourgeois, identified three main models of suicidal behaviour in animals available to ecological research: (a) “unfavourable dispersions due to demographic pressures or human encroachment”; (b) “altruistic sacrifice to protect the group”; (c) “grief over loss of a beloved owner. The first mechanism is unlikely to offer important analogies to human suicidal behaviour; the other two models are plausible but rare in humans.

To date, naturalists have not identified suicide in nonhuman species in field situations despite intensive study of thousands of animal species. Suicide is a rare occurrence in humans: No surprise then if evidence of suicidal animals in both wild and domestic situations is hard to find. However, there is sparse evidence of self-harming behaviours in animals.

Animals subject to sudden confinement and pets experiencing separation from their owners sometimes show self-destructive behaviours. Stressful situations such as crowding, isolation, separation, and confinement, especially when perceived as uncontrollable, can result in self-endangering behaviours, particularly among primates. The impairing effects caused by internal parasites on behaviour are thought to stimulate self-endangering behaviours in some species of insects, such as butterflies, and of mammals, such as the “suicidal” attraction to the feline in rodents parasitized by toxoplasma gondii. Overall, these instances are rather doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing.

Among birds, strong competition is observed among siblings to gain parental resources. It was proposed that as mortality by starvation increases, selection would favour fratricide at first, then fratricide plus infanticide, and finally “suicide” by the nestling with the shortest life expectancy.

Besides these cases of extreme self-sacrifice, the instances when an animal endangers its life to the point of risking death generally fall within parental behaviours or actions done on behalf of a close kin. Altruistic suicide is known in humans. Suicide with hostile intent, aimed at revenge, is recorded in the anthropological literature, and spiteful or retaliation intentions are sometimes encoded in suicide notes. Nevertheless, altruistic suicide and suicide with hostile intent form a small fraction of all cases of suicides: The majority of suicides can be attributed to the will to stop unbearable suffering or to detach oneself from life circumstances.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_9.question_text: Based on the passage, which statement best reflects the scientific consensus on suicide in animals?
options: Animals, especially domesticated ones, are capable of suicide.
There is no evidence on whether animals in the wild display self-harming behaviours.
Only humans have been conclusively shown to be affected by unbearable suffering.
Animals sometimes display self-harm behaviours, but they are not equivalent to human suicides.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_9.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The passage outlines various scenarios where animals, both domestic and wild, exhibit behaviours that could be interpreted as self-harming or even suicidal. Phrases like “sparse evidence of selfharming behaviours in animals” and “stressful situations such as crowding, isolation, separation, and confinement, especially when perceived as uncontrollable, can result in self-endangering behaviours” point to this. However, the text also makes it explicitly clear that these behaviours are “doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing.” In essence, while there are behaviours in animals that may resemble self-harm or suicide, these actions are not equivalent to human suicides, according to the scientific consensus. Therefore, option (4), “Animals sometimes display selfharm behaviours, but they are not equivalent to human suicides,” aligns most accurately with the information presented in the passage.

Incorrect Answers:
1. The passage does detail situations where domestic animals, like dogs, may appear to starve themselves to death after the passing of an owner or mate. However, it goes on to clarify that “To date, naturalists have not identified suicide in nonhuman species in field situations despite intensive study of thousands of animal species.” So, while the behaviour might be perceived as self-destructive, it hasn’t been scientifically classified as suicide. This makes option (1), “Animals, especially domesticated ones, are capable of suicide,” incorrect according to the scientific consensus in the passage.
2. Option (2), “There is no evidence on whether animals in the wild display self-harming behaviours,” is directly contradicted by the passage. The text clearly states that there is “sparse evidence of self-harming behaviours in animals.” Although the evidence is limited, it does exist, making the claim that there is “no evidence” incorrect.
3. The statement “Only humans have been conclusively shown to be affected by unbearable suffering” is not directly addressed in the passage. The focus is more on the concept of suicide and selfharming behaviours in animals, not on the psychology of suffering. The passage doesn’t offer a comparative analysis between human and animal suffering to conclude that only humans are affected by unbearable suffering. Therefore, option (3) is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_10.question_text: Which among the following facts, if true, would cast the most doubt on the rationale given for attributing suicidal behaviour to animals based on anecdotal examples like grieving dogs and geese?
options:
Animals have been observed to adopt maladaptive behaviours for other reasons, such as resource
scarcity.

Animals’ “self-destructive” behaviours often have underlying evolutionary benefits that are not
immediately obvious.
Most animals that display self-destructive behaviours recover once stressors are removed.

The studies on suicidal behaviours in animals are largely inconclusive due to the small sample
sizes.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_10.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The passage discusses various instances where animals, such as grieving dogs and geese, seem to display self-destructive or self-harming behaviours. It’s implied that these behaviours are commonly interpreted as evidence of “suicidal” tendencies. However, if it’s true that these “self-destructive” behaviours actually have underlying evolutionary benefits that are not immediately obvious, it would challenge the rationale behind attributing suicidal intent to these actions. If these actions serve some other evolutionary purpose, they may not accurately be termed “suicidal” according to the human understanding of the term. Therefore, option (2) would cast the most doubt on the rationale for attributing suicidal behaviour to animals based on anecdotal examples.

Incorrect Answers:

1. While the notion that animals may adopt maladaptive behaviours for other reasons, such as resource scarcity, could add another layer to the discussion, it doesn’t directly challenge the rationale behind attributing these behaviours to suicidal intent. Such maladaptive behaviours might still be misinterpreted as suicidal, making this option less effective at casting doubt on the rationale.
3. The fact that most animals displaying self-destructive behaviours recover once stressors are removed could be seen as evidence against the concept of suicide in animals. However, it doesn’t necessarily negate the possibility that the animals were exhibiting suicidal tendencies during the period of stress. Thus, while it adds nuance, it doesn’t fundamentally challenge the rationale of attributing suicidal intent based on anecdotal evidence.
4. Although studies being inconclusive due to small sample sizes could weaken the overall argument for or against animal suicide, it doesn’t specifically challenge the rationale behind the anecdotal attributions of suicidal behaviour to grieving dogs and geese. The issue here is not the size of the sample but the interpretation of the behaviour itself.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_11.question_text: The last sentence of the fifth paragraph states, “Overall, these instances are rather doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing.” This conclusion rests on the assumption that ________.
options: all species of animals share an equivalent level of emotional complexity as humans do.

human suicidal behaviour is an inappropriate model for understanding suicidal tendencies in
other animals.

manifestations of self-destructive behaviours are limited exclusively to domesticated animal
species.

self-endangering behaviours in animals lack the intentional thought process that characterizes
human suicide.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_11.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The fifth paragraph concludes with the statement, “Overall, these instances are rather doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing.” This conclusion is drawn after discussing various scenarios where animals might display self-harming or self-destructive behaviours. The conclusion aims to distinguish these animal behaviours from human suicide. For this conclusion to be valid, there must be a fundamental difference that separates human suicide from similar animal behaviours. The assumption is that this difference is the level of intentionality involved. Human suicides are intentional acts, while the self-endangering behaviours in animals lack this level of conscious planning or intent. Thus, the necessity of this assumption lies in its ability to create a distinguishing factor that validates the conclusion of the passage. Therefore, option (4), “self-endangering behaviours in animals lack the intentional thought process that characterizes human suicide,” most closely captures this necessary assumption, making it the correct answer.

Incorrect Answers:
1. The passage does not assume that all species of animals share an equivalent level of emotional complexity as humans do. In fact, the passage seems to imply that there is a difference in the complexities of behaviour between humans and animals, specifically when it comes to the intention behind self-destructive acts. Therefore, the argument does not hinge on this idea, making option (1) incorrect.
2. The passage actually uses human suicide as a comparison point for examining self-destructive behaviours in animals. It attempts to measure the validity of claiming that animals can be suicidal by comparing their actions to the known, intentional act of suicide in humans. Therefore, it’s not assuming that human suicide is an inappropriate model; rather, it’s using it as a framework to evaluate animal behaviour. Option (2) is not an assumption underlying the conclusion.
3. The passage discusses both domesticated animals and animals in the wild, making it clear that the occurrence (or lack thereof) of self-destructive behaviours is not limited to a particular category of animals. The argument’s conclusion does not rely on this idea, so option (3) is incorrect.

options:
Highlighting the limitations and challenges in drawing parallels between a phenomenon in
nonhuman animals and similar behaviour in humans.

Providing evidence, both scientific and anecdotal, to prove that a specific phenomenon cannot
be conclusively found in any animals.

Analyzing a phenomenon in nonhuman animals through the lens of human behaviour and

Arguing that a phenomenon observed in humans is not present in nonhuman animals, based on
both scientific and non-scientific exploration.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_12.solution:
Solution:

It points out limitations in drawing direct parallels between these behaviours in nonhuman animals and humans. The text states that “these instances are rather doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing,” which emphasizes the complexities and challenges in making such comparisons. Therefore, option (1) best captures the primary focus of the passage, making it the correct answer.

Incorrect Answers:
2. The passage does discuss scientific and anecdotal evidence related to self-destructive behaviours in animals but does not aim to prove that this phenomenon “cannot be conclusively found in any animals.” Importantly, the passage explicitly mentions that humans, who are also animals, do commit suicide. Therefore, this option is not supported by the text.
3. While the passage does examine self-destructive behaviours in nonhuman animals through the lens of human behaviour, it doesn’t conclude that such an analysis is valid. The phrase “doubtful parallels with human intentional self-killing” serves as an indication that the passage does not validate this method of analysis.

4. The passage doesn’t argue that the phenomenon observed in humans is not present in nonhuman animals. Therefore, option (4) does not accurately represent the main focus of the passage and is incorrect.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_13.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16: The passage below is accompanied by a set of questions. Choose the best answer to each question.

Sir Thomas More was the first person to use the term “utopia,” describing an ideal, imaginary world in his most famous work of fiction. His book describes a complex community on an island in which people share a common culture and way of life. The term he coined derives from the Greek word ou-topos, meaning “nowhere,”. Ironically, it is the opposite of the similar-sounding Greek word eu-topos, meaning “a good place”. At its heart, the book poses the question of whether there could ever be such a thing as a “perfect” world and served as a platform to highlight the chaos of European politics at the time. . . .

The society depicted in Utopia differs from the European society that Thomas More was living in at the time, one rife with intrigue, corruption and mired by scandal. The author’s experience with politics in his time and the Utopia that he invented demonstrates this contrasting relationship: Utopia is communal, allowing its people to easily meet their needs, while European society is described as a place where “Idle monarchs and nobles seek to increase their own wealth and power at the expense of the people, who are left in poverty and misery”. Clearly dissatisfied with the world he was living in, More sought to create a different place altogether on the page—a world free of the hierarchies that ultimately cost the author his life. . . .

The story unfolds as Giles introduces More to Raphael Hythloday, a well-travelled philosopher. Engaged in a debate on the intersection of philosophy and politics in Book One, More and Giles are captivated by Hythloday’s insights and propose that he serve as an advisor to the king. cl_2023_cdc_11_q_14.question_text: Which one of the following cannot be inferred from the passage?
options: Utopia serves as an allegory to expose the vices and inequities in European society.
The lack of private property in Utopia is a cornerstone for its societal equality.
More, Giles, and Hythloday all agree that Utopia's system is superior to European models.
None of the options above.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_14.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Correct Answer is option 3. This statement is not supported by the passage. While it is evident that Hythloday considers Utopia's system to be superior to European models, it is not clear that More and Giles fully agree. The passage mentions that they find "some aspects of Utopia perplexing and irrational," though they concede that "certain elements could be beneficial if integrated into European societies." This suggests that More and Giles are intrigued but not necessarily in full agreement that Utopia's system is superior. Therefore, this option is correct as it cannot be inferred from the passage.

Incorrect Answers:
Option (1) : The passage describes that Utopia is communal and allows its people to easily meet their needs, contrasting it with European society, which is described as rife with "intrigue, corruption" and a place where people are "left in poverty and misery." This serves to expose the vices and inequities in European society, making this option incorrect as an answer to the question of what cannot be inferred.”
Option (2) The passage states that in Utopia, "there is no greed, corruption, or power struggles due to the fact that there is no money or private property." This suggests that the lack of private property is essential for the societal equality found in Utopia. Therefore, this statement can be inferred from the passage and is incorrect.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_15.question_text: Which one of the following best describes the motivation behind More’s book Utopia?
options:
The book serves as a historical account of Thomas More’s personal experiences in European
politics, aiming to document the political landscape at that time.

Through the lens of an ideal society, the book draws attention to the flaws and corruption
plaguing European politics during Thomas More’s era.

While discussing an imaginary Utopia, the objective is to offer readers an escapist fantasy
against the prevalent corrupt political environment in Europe,

The book aims to initiate a philosophical debate about the practicality of Utopian concepts in
real-world politics, advocating the adoption of a society that promotes equality.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_15.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The passage makes it clear that Thomas More’s book “Utopia” serves primarily as an allegorical critique of European society during his time. It states, “Clearly dissatisfied with the world he was living in, More sought to create a different place altogether on the page—a world free of the hierarchies that ultimately cost the author his life.” This suggests that the book is a reaction to the flaws and corruption present in European politics. By describing a communal and equitable society in “Utopia,” More implicitly highlights the issues plaguing the European society in which he lived, such as greed, corruption, and social inequality.

Incorrect Answers:
1. Although the passage mentions that Thomas More had experiences with European politics, there’s no indication that “Utopia” was intended to serve as a historical account of his personal experiences or to document the political landscape. The book is portrayed more as an allegorical critique rather than a historical account.
3. The book does discuss an imaginary Utopia, but the passage does not suggest that the objective is to offer readers an “escapist fantasy.” Rather, the depiction of Utopia is used to make a serious point about the flaws in European society.
4. While the book does touch on philosophical debates, especially through the characters of More, Giles, and Hythloday, the passage does not indicate that the primary aim is to initiate a philosophical debate about the practicality of Utopian concepts. It’s more focused on highlighting the shortcomings of European society as opposed to advocating for a specific societal model.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_16.question_text: Each of the following, if false, would call into question the logic of Utopian society EXCEPT:
options: ethical governance would naturally lead to a more equitable society.
a communal system of property ownership can solve many of society’s problems.
when provided with the right conditions, human beings will act in ways that benefit society.
citizens having a shared belief in society’s principles need the incorporation of punitive measures.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_16.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The original question is, “Each of the following, if false, would call into question the logic of Utopian society EXCEPT.” This employs a triple negative: “if false,” “call into question,” and “EXCEPT.” Counteracting these negatives, the question can be rephrased as, “Which of the following, if true, would call into question the logic of Utopian society?”
Given the reframed question, we are looking for a statement that, if true, would challenge the logic of Utopia as described in the passage.
Option (4) directly challenges the Utopian notion of a society operating smoothly through shared principles and ethical governance. This is because the Utopian ideal assumes that shared beliefs and communal living automatically ensure societal harmony, making punitive measures unnecessary. According to the passage, Utopia functions largely without conflict or the need for punitive measures based on its ethical and equitable foundation. If option (4) were true, it would fundamentally undermine the Utopian claim that a society can function well without punitive mechanisms to enforce compliance. Therefore, it is the most suitable answer.

Incorrect Answers:
1. This option aligns well with the Utopian ideals described in the passage. Utopia is portrayed as a model of ethical governance, which in turn fosters social equality. If this statement were true, it would serve to support, not undermine, the core principles of Utopian society. If this were false, it would undermine the notion of ethical governance in Utopia, thus calling its principles into question.
2. This option directly mirrors the Utopian value of communal property ownership, which is described as one of society’s foundational elements. Proving this true would actually bolster the case for Utopian society rather than weaken it. But if false, this would call into question one of the foundational aspects of the Utopian society described in the passage.
3. This statement is consonant with the portrayal of Utopia in the passage. In a Utopian setting, the ‘right conditions’ are seemingly provided for everyone, leading to a society where citizens act for the collective good. Proving this statement true would fortify, not question, the Utopian paradigm. But if it were false, it would undermine this assumption and thus call Utopian principles into question.

Elkins-Tanton, vice president of Arizona State University’s Interplanetary Initiative and foundation and regents professor in the School of Earth and Space Exploration there, is the lead scientist for NASA’s Psyche mission, scheduled to launch on October 12. __2__. The mission plans to land on the unusually metallic asteroid Psyche to gain clues about the cores and formations of other planets and moons—including ours. __3__. As an insecure young girl reading the Polish poet Czes³aw Mi³osz and building her own bicycle, she may not have looked much like a future scientific leader. __4__. For one thing, she was a woman. Yet she has always been a seeker, someone on a quest.
options: Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_18.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
• The paragraph starts by introducing Linda Elkins-Tanton and her current role as the lead scientist for NASA’s Psyche mission, outlining the mission’s objectives.
• The second segment follows by delving into the technical details of the Psyche mission, discussing what the mission plans to achieve.
• The third segment provides a contrast to the preceding information, describing Elkins-Tanton as an insecure young girl interested in poetry and bicycle-building.
• The last part of the paragraph mentions another challenge she faced, which is being a woman in a typically male-dominated field, concluding that she has always been a seeker on a quest.
Given this flow:
Option 1 comes right after introducing Linda Elkins-Tanton’s current professional life. Inserting the sentence here could disrupt the flow of the paragraph, which initially aims to focus on her current roles and mission.
Option 2 follows the details of the Psyche mission, and placing the sentence here could seem incongruent as it doesn’t connect well with the mission’s details.
Option 3 is situated right after describing her as an insecure young girl with varied interests. Inserting the sentence here serves as a smooth transition, connecting her past and present and adding depth to her character. It acts as a bridge that reveals the complex path leading to her current role.
Option 4 comes after discussing the challenges she faced, specifically being a woman in a maledominated field. Inserting the sentence here might lose the impact of directly contrasting her past and present roles.
Thus, Option 3 is the most appropriate place for the sentence, “Yet Elkins-Tanton’s path to success— and to science—took many twists and turns.” It serves as a transition that enriches the narrative by connecting her early life and interests to her current significant role in the scientific community.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_19.question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

South Africa’s African Renaissance is a multi-faceted concept that is less popular than Mandela’s Rainbow Nation metaphor, partly due to its complexity. It intersects with Black Economic Empowerment (BEE), a government initiative designed to boost the economic participation of black South Africans. The concept also invites criticism for its irony and contradictions, such as the idea that South Africa, once isolated from Africa during apartheid, should spearhead an “African” renaissance. Additionally, the term “Renaissance” itself is European in origin, which seems to contradict the movement’s aim to break from European colonial legacy. Another point of contention is the movement’s reliance on English despite South Africa’s eleven official languages, nine of which are Bantu languages. Critics argue that this linguistic choice may alienate the majority of South Africans, who do not speak English as their mother tongue.
options:
South Africa’s African Renaissance is a complex concept riddled with ironies and contradictions,
including its European terminology and reliance on English.

The African Renaissance concept intersects with Black Economic Empowerment, suggesting
that they share some objectives but may also have unique complications.

The reliance on English could potentially alienate people who do not speak the language, raising
questions about inclusivity within the African Renaissance movement.

South Africa’s leading role in the African Renaissance raises questions, given its historic isolation
from the African continent during the apartheid era.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_19.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Your Answer : 1
Option 1 is the most comprehensive summary of the passage. It captures the essential points that the concept of South Africa’s African Renaissance is complex and fraught with ironies and contradictions. Specifically, the summary mentions the irony of using a European term, “Renaissance,” in a movement aimed at breaking free from the European colonial legacy. Additionally, it alludes to the contradiction of relying on English, which could potentially alienate many South Africans. Hence, this option covers multiple dimensions of the issues discussed in the passage.
Incorrect Answers:
2. This option is incorrect because it only discusses the intersection with Black Economic Empowerment (BEE). While this is an aspect of the passage, it does not capture its full essence, which includes the complexities and contradictions inherent in the African Renaissance concept.
3. This option narrows down the focus of the passage to just the language issue. While the linguistic choice is indeed a point of contention, it’s only one facet of the larger complexities and contradictions surrounding the African Renaissance concept. Therefore, this summary is incomplete.
4. This option focuses solely on South Africa’s controversial position as a leader in the African Renaissance, given its historical isolation from Africa. Like the other incorrect options, this one, too, is partial and does not capture the full scope of complexities and contradictions presented in the passage.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_23.question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Twenty-three hundred years ago, Aristotle concluded that, more than anything else, men and women seek happiness. While happiness itself is sought for its own sake, every other goal—health, beauty, money, or power—is valued only because we expect that it will make us happy. Much has changed since Aristotle’s time. Our understanding of the worlds of stars and of atoms has expanded beyond belief. The gods of the Greeks were like helpless children compared to humankind today and the powers we now wield. And yet, on this most important issue, very little has changed in the intervening centuries. We do not understand what happiness is any better than Aristotle did, and as for learning how to attain that blessed condition, one could argue that we have made no progress at all.
options: The quest for happiness is a constant across time, from Aristotle to the present day, but we have gained many other things like money and power that make us happy.
The gods of ancient Greece were childlike compared to the human powers of today, and yet we have not improved in our understanding of what happiness is.
Despite profound changes in human capabilities and understanding, humankind's understanding of happiness remains as limited as it was in Aristotle's time.

Aristotle believed that all goals, such as health, beauty, money, or power, are aimed at achieving happiness, and this belief still holds true today, but our understanding of happiness has not improved.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_23.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
Option (3) most accurately captures the essence of the passage for several reasons. First, it identifies the continuity of the human quest for happiness, a concept that was first articulated by Aristotle over two millennia ago. Second, it underscores the contrast that, although there have been significant advancements in human capabilities and general understanding of the world—implied by references to expanded knowledge of stars and atoms and increased human power—the core issue of what happiness is and how to achieve it has remained fundamentally unaltered. This focus on the dichotomy between human progress in various fields and the stagnation in understanding happiness aligns perfectly with the main thrust of the passage, making option (3) the most comprehensive and accurate summary.

Incorrect Answers:
(1) The key issue with this option is that it implies that the acquisition of money and power has contributed to happiness. The passage, however, makes no such claim. It only says that health, beauty, money, or power are pursued because we expect they will contribute to happiness, not that they actually do. Therefore, this summary introduces a point that is not in the original text.
(2) This option touches on the contrast between ancient beliefs and modern capabilities but focuses more on the gods of ancient Greece. It misses out on the broader point of the lack of progress in understanding happiness. The comparison with gods serves only as a measure of how far we have come in other aspects but not in understanding happiness, which is the passage's main point.
(4) Option 4 comes close to capturing the essence of the passage but has a slight nuance that makes it less accurate. While it talks about Aristotle's belief that all goals aim for happiness, it unnecessarily extends this belief to hold true today, which the passage does not explicitly state. The passage primarily focuses on how little our understanding of happiness has changed over time, not on whether Aristotle's specific beliefs still hold true.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_24.question_text:
There is a sentence that is missing in the paragraph below. Look at the paragraph and decide in which blank (option 1, 2, 3, or 4) the following sentence would best fit.

Sentence: Getting married is a rather complicated business.

Paragraph: __1__. Our society expects us all to get married. With only rare exceptions we all do just that. __2__. It involves mastering certain complex hustling and courtship games, the rituals and the ceremonies that celebrate the act of marriage, and finally, the difficult requirements of domestic life with a husband or wife. __3__. It is an enormously elaborate round of activity, much more so than finding a job, and yet while many resolutely remain unemployed, few remain unmarried. __4__.
options: Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_24.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Your Answer : 2
• The paragraph begins by establishing a societal expectation that people should get married, stating that with few exceptions, most people do end up getting married.
• The next segment goes into the complexities involved in getting married, discussing the rituals, ceremonies, and domestic life challenges that come with it.
• The third segment contrasts the complexity of marriage with finding a job, pointing out that while some may choose not to work, fewer opt to remain unmarried.
• The paragraph ends with an open space for additional information or conclusions.
Given this flow:
Option 1 is at the very beginning, where the societal expectation of marriage is discussed. Placing the sentence here might be a bit abrupt as the paragraph starts by talking about societal norms, not the complexity of the act.
Option 2 comes right after stating that most people conform to the societal expectation of getting married. Inserting the sentence here provides a smooth transition into discussing the complexities involved in marriage, serving as a bridge between the expectation and the reality.
Option 3 follows the comparison between the complexity of marriage and the act of finding a job. Inserting the sentence here would disrupt the flow as it interrupts a point of comparison.
Option 4 is at the end of the paragraph, which may not be the best fit for this sentence as it would fail to set the stage for discussing the complexities detailed in the middle of the paragraph.
Therefore, Option 2 is the most suitable place for the sentence, “Getting married is a rather complicated business.” It transitions smoothly from the initial point about societal expectations to delve into the intricate details and rituals that come with marriage.

Correct Answer : 4

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_47.question_text:
In a math class the students are asked to pick an odd number (say N) less than 6 and write it on the board. Then they are asked to perform the following operations:
(1) If N is a perfect square, find its positive square root. Write it on the board.
(2) If N is not a perfect square:

(a) Add N to the number. Write the number on the board.
(b) If the new number is a perfect square, find its positive square root. Write it on the board.
(c) If it is not a perfect square repeat the process from 2(a).

At any point if the same set of numbers is repeating, the student can stop the operation.

Find the average of all the unique numbers obtained by the children.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_47.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
The numbers picked are {1, 3, 5}.
The number 1 is a perfect square, so in each step the number 1 is only obtained.
For N = 3, we get (3, 6, 9) and then they are repeated.

For N = 5, we get (5, 10, 15, 20, 25) and then they are repeated. So the distinct numbers obtained by each child are {1, 3, 6, 9, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25}. There are 9 distinct numbers.
Hence, required average = 94/9.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_53.question_text: How many 6-digit natural numbers containing only the digits 1, 3, and 5 are there in which 5 occurs at least twice and the number is divisible by both 3 and 5?
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_53.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 71
Since the number must be divisible by 5, therefore, the last digit must be 5.
Case 1: 5 occurs exactly twice:
For example: 5 … 5
Since number is divisible by 3, therefore, sum of the digits should be atleast 15, 18, 21, 24 and so on… So, number must be of the form 533115 or similar.
Such possible numbers are 5!/(2! × 2!) = 30.
Case 2: 5 occurs exactly thrice:
For example: 5 … 5 5
Since number is divisible by 3, therefore, number should be of the form:
511155, 533355
Such possible numbers are 5!/(2! × 3!) + 5!/(2! × 3!) = 20.
Case 3: 5 occurs exactly four times:
For example: 5 … 5 5 5
Number divisible by 3 : 513555
Such possible numbers are 5!/3! = 20.
Case 4: 5 occurs five times will give no number divisible by 3.
Case 5: 5 occurs six times.
The number will be 555555 and it will be divisible by 3.
Hence, total numbers possible are = 71.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_56.question_text: The increasing sequence 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, … , consists of all positive integers that are neither the square nor the cube of a positive integer. The 100th term of the sequence is:
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_56.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 112
112 = 121 is the smallest square of a natural number greater than 100.
There are 11 squares and 4 cubes less than or equal to 121, but 1 and 64 are both square and cube.
So there are 121 – 11 – 4 + 2 = 108 numbers in our sequence less than 121.
So 108th term of the sequence = 120
Hence, 100th term of the sequence = 120 – 8 = 112.

Shortcut: 100th term = 100 + 10 + 4 – 2 = 112.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_57.question_text: There are four salesmen - A, B, C, and D. In the first month, they received a total commission of Rs.21,000 from their company and divided it in the ratio 2 : 3 : 4 : 5. In the next month, the commission tripled and the amount was divided in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5 : 2. What was the ratio of the total commission earned by A and D during the two months respectively?
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_57.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The total amounts (in Rs.) received in 1st and 2nd months are 21000 and 63000 respectively.
Total amount received by A = 2/14 × 21000 + 3/14 × 63000 = Rs. 16,500
Total amount received by D = 5/14 × 21000 + 2/14 × 63000 = Rs. 16,500
Hence, the required ratio = 1 : 1.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_59.question_text: Priya, Rita, and Diya working alone can do a piece of work in 20, 30, and 60 hours respectively. All three of them start the work together, but after x hours Priya leaves and then after y more hours Rita leaves and Diya completes the remaining work. If Rita had not left, Diya and Rita would have completed the remaining work in (y + 6) hours after Priya had left. If both Priya and Rita had stayed, the work would have been completed in (x + 6) hours. What is the total number of hours taken to complete the work?
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_59.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 28
Priya, Rita and Diya together complete:
(1/20 + 1/30 + 1/60) = 1/10 th of the work per hour.
The work will be completed by the three of them together in 10 hours.
Given that together, the three take (x + 6) hours to complete the work
⇒ x + 6 = 10 ⇒ x = 4
The work remaining after Priya leaves can be completed by Rita and Diya in (y + 6) hours.
∴ (y + 6)(1/30 + 1/60) = (1 – x/10)
⇒ (y + 6) 3/60 = [1 – (4/10)] ⇒ y = 6 hours.
It is given that Priya left after x hours and Rita left y hours after that and remaining work was completed by Diya.
Remaining work = 1 – 4/10 – 6(1/30 + 1/60) = 3/10
Time taken by Diya to complete 3/10 th of the work = (3/10)/(1/60) = 18 hours.
Hence, time taken by the three of them to complete the work = 4 + 6 + 18 = 28 hours.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_61.question_text: Two motor boats, V and W, start from a point on a river and follow the same route. V travels downstream at 10 m/s, while W starts 5 minutes later at 7 m/s downstream. Fifteen minutes after W has started, two passengers on boat V decide that they need to join their family members on boat W. They are left behind on a raft that floats along the river to be picked up by boat W. If the speed of the river's current is 2 m/s, find the time (in minutes) for which the passengers stayed on the raft.
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_61.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Boat V travels at a speed of 10 m/s downstream.
By the time the two passengers on boat V decide to get off, their boat will have traveled for 20 minutes.
It covers 20 × 60 × 10 = 12000 m, while boat W travels for 15 minutes and covers a distance = 15 × 60 × 7 = 6300 m
So the raft is (12000 – 6300) = 5700 m ahead of boat W.
Boat W gains (7 – 2) = 5 m/s with respect to the raft.
Hence, it takes 5700/5 = 1140 seconds = 19 minutes for W to reach the raft.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_64.question_text: Ten identical chocolates are to be distributed among five children - V, W, X, Y, and Z. What is the number of ways of distributing it so that only two children get exactly two chocolates each while the rest could get any number of chocolates?
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_64.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
There are 10 ways to pick two children out of V, W, X, Y, and Z such that they get 2 chocolates each. We need to distribute the remaining (10 – 2 – 2) = 6 chocolates among the remaining three children such that none of them get exactly 2 chocolates.

The possible distributions are (6 + 0 + 0) (which can be done in 3 ways)
or (5 + 1 + 0) (which can be done in 6 ways)
or (4 + 1 + 1) (which can be done in 3 ways)
or (3 + 3 + 0) (which can be done in 3 ways).
Hence, the required answer is 10 × (3 + 6 + 3 + 3) = 150.

cl_2023_cdc_11_q_65.question_text: Tanu and Sana start from their homes at 9:30 AM and 12:00 PM respectively, in the same direction, to meet their other friends. Tanu moves at a speed of 7 km/h while Sana moves at the speed of 12 km/h. If Sana overtakes Tanu, then what time was it?
options: 4 PM
cl_2023_cdc_11_q_65.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The distance covered by Tanu in 2 hour 30 minutes = 7 + 7 + 7 × 1/2 = 17.5 km
Thus, the time taken by Sana to overtake Tanu = 17.5/(12 – 7) = 17.5/5 = 3.5 hours
Hence, Sana will overtake Tanu at 12 PM + 3 hours + 30 minutes = 3:30 PM.
